title-group.article-title: Efficacy of esketamine in reducing nausea and vomiting after anesthesia: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
title-group.alt-title: Efficacy of Esketamine on Gi: Rct Review

contrib-group.aff: Department of Anesthesiology, The Affiliated Children’s Hospital of Xiangya School of Medicine, Central South University (Hunan children’s hospital), Changsha, China

fn.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/07853890.2025.2604409.

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Shuangquan Qu shuangquanqu@126.com Hunan Children’s Hospital, 86 Ziyuan Road, Yuhua District, Changsha City, Hunan Province, China.

event.event-desc: published online in a building issue

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) are significant perioperative challenges. This study evaluated the efficacy of perioperative esketamine in preventing PONV.

sec.title: Materials and methods
sec.p: We systematically searched Embase, PubMed, Web of Science, and the Cochrane Library from inception to August 2025 for randomized controlled trials investigating the effect of perioperative esketamine on PONV. The primary outcome was PONV incidence. Secondary outcomes included time to first flatus, postoperative pain degree, anxiety scores, agitation, anesthesia recovery time, and post-anesthesia care unit (PACU) stay duration. Data were analyzed using RevMan 5.4 and STATA 15.0 software. Sensitivity and subgroup analyses were performed to assess result stability and explore potential sources of heterogeneity.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Thirty-eight randomized trials (3,425 patients) were included. Esketamine reduced the risk of nausea (RR=0.69, 95% CI: 0.53–0.90) and vomiting (RR=0.75, 95% CI: 0.57–0.98), shortened time to first flatus (SMD=–0.81, 95% CI: −1.48 to –0.15), and decreased rescue analgesic needs within 2 days (SMD=0.32, 95% CI: 0.2–0.5). However, it prolonged anesthesia recovery time (SMD=0.97, 95% CI: 0.28–1.67) and PACU stay (SMD=0.76, 95% CI: 0.27–1.26).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Perioperative esketamine may reduce PONV and aid gastrointestinal recovery, but its potential to delay anesthesia recovery and PACU discharge requires consideration. Further studies are needed to clarify its risk-benefit profile.

sec.title: Date of first submission to PROSPERO
sec.p: 10 March 2024

sec.title: Date of the start of study screening against eligibility criteria
sec.p: 21 March 2024

institution-wrap.institution: Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of Medical and Healthcare

funding-group.funding-statement: This research was supported by the Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of Medical and Healthcare Industry Joint Fund Project: Mechanism study on the modulation of SRPK1 succinylation modification by esketamine through SIRT5 in acute lung injury in sepsis (Project No. 2024JJ9342)

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) is a common complication following general anesthesia and surgery, and is closely associated with prolonged hospital stays, increased healthcare costs, and reduced patient satisfaction[1,2]. Despite various preventive measures, the reported incidence of PONV remains high, ranging from 20% to 60%. According to the Apfel risk scoring system, female sex, non-smoking status, history of motion sickness or PONV, and postoperative opioid use are key risk factors [3–5]; patients with three or four of these factors are considered high risk for PONV.

The type of surgery itself is an important determinant of PONV risk. Generally, longer surgical duration and greater tissue trauma correlate with a higher risk of PONV. For instance, abdominal surgeries (particularly laparoscopic procedures) significantly increase PONV risk due to direct manipulation and traction of the gastrointestinal tract, which can readily induces gastrointestinal dysfunction. Gynecological surgeries are also considered high-risk, influenced by both the patient population (predominantly female) and the surgical site characteristics. Furthermore, surgeries involving the head, neck, eyes, or ears may induce PONV by stimulating vestibular pathways. In contrast, superficial or limb surgeries typically carry a lower PONV risk.

Recovery of postoperative gastrointestinal function is influenced by multiple factors, including the surgical approach, extent of trauma, and perioperative opioid use [6,7]. Opioids, by stimulating chemoreceptors and inhibiting gastrointestinal motility, are key contributors to PONV and gastrointestinal dysfunction [8–11]. To mitigate these effects, opioid-free anesthesia (OFA) has been proposed as an innovative multimodal strategy. This approach typically combines α-2 agonists, N-methyl-d-aspartate (NMDA) receptor antagonists, and local anesthetics to avoid opioid-related complications [12,13].

In this context, esketamine (the S(+)-enantiomer of ketamine) has garnered considerable attention due to its higher affinity for NMDA receptors compared to racemic ketamine, providing equivalent analgesic efficacy at lower doses with potentially reduced side effects [14]. However, recent clinical studies on esketamine have yielded conflicting results. Some reports indicate that esketamine-based OFA improves gastrointestinal recovery and reduces PONV in gynecological laparoscopic procedures [15]. In contrast, other studies have shown that even when esketamine is combined with low-dose opioids—resulting in a substantial reduction in opioid consumption—it did not significantly decrease PONV incidence [16]. These divergent findings underscore the urgent need for a systematic evaluation of esketamine’s role in the perioperative period.

Currently, no systematic evaluation exists on the efficacy and safety of esketamine in managing postoperative gastrointestinal function. This study therefore aims to evaluate the impact of perioperative esketamine on PONV and gastrointestinal function through a systematic review and meta-analysis, to provide evidence-based guidance for clinical practice.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This study was registered with PROSPERO (CRD42024522935) on March 10, 2024. We adhered to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines [17] for reporting outcome.

sec.title: Literature search strategy
sec.p: A systematic literature search was implemented across major medical databases (Embase, PubMed, Web of Science, and the Cochrane Library) through August 2025, without a lower date limit. The search combined MeSH terminology and free-text words related to ‘esketamine’, ‘ketamine’, ‘gastrointestinal dysfunction’, ‘nausea’, ‘vomit’, ‘diarrhea’, ‘abdominal pain’ and ‘anesthesia’. Additional eligible studies were identified through a manual review of reference lists from relevant publications. The complete search strategy for one database is available in Supplementary Table S1.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: The study selection process involved a two-stage screening approach. During the initial screening, two reviewers (LT & JW) independently evaluated article titles and abstracts. Following this, full manuscripts of potentially relevant papers underwent detailed eligibility assessment. Discrepancies in selection decisions were addressed through discussion or arbitration by a senior researcher (SQQ).

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: Studies were selected based upon the following PICO framework:

sec.title: Population
sec.p: Surgical patients receiving perioperative anesthesia.

sec.title: Intervention
sec.p: Perioperative administration of esketamine (either as bolus doses or continuous infusion).

sec.title: Comparator
sec.p: Standard anesthetic care or placebo (including normal saline or conventional narcotic drugs).

sec.title: Outcomes
sec.p: Primary outcomes included nausea and vomiting. Secondary outcomes included time to first flatus, requirement for rescue analgesia, postoperative recovery time, PACU stay duration, pain scores, the incidence of adverse effects such as dizziness and agitation, and perioperative alterations in heart rate, mean arterial pressure, and blood oxygen saturation. The inclusion criteria specified peer-reviewed RCTs published in English. The analysis excluded non-randomized designs, including case reports, observational research, editorial materials, literature reviews, meeting abstracts, and studies examining procedural sedation.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Study information was extracted using a pre-designed template. Key variables collected included publication date, authorship, geographical location, trial methodology, age, sex, body mass index, sample size, intervention, esketamine administration protocol, dosing regimen, time of operation, time of anesthesia, time of recovery and postoperative follow-up, and gastrointestinal symptoms including abdominal distension, abdominal pain, vomiting, nausea. Two investigators (LT & JW) independently performed data extraction. Disagreements in data extraction were adjudicated by a third investigator (SQ Q). For cases with incomplete or ambiguous information, study authors were approached for additional details.

sec.title: Outcome and subgroup analysis
sec.p: We used vomiting and nausea within 48 h post-surgery as our primary outcomes. Secondary outcomes included abdominal distension, time to first flatus and defecation, perioperative parameters (operative duration, anesthesia time, recovery time, PACU stay duration), postoperative pain scores, agitation, vertigo, and patient satisfaction scores. Subgroup analyses were stratified by surgical type, postoperative follow-up duration, comparison of anesthetic agents, geographical location of study, time of nausea and vomiting, and time to first flatus and defecation.

sec.title: Methodological quality assessment
sec.p: The study quality assessment followed the guidelines of the Cochrane Risk of Bias Tool [18], with two reviewers (L T&J W) conducting independent evaluations. Any disagreements in quality assessment were decided by the third author (SQ Q). The methodological quality of each study was assessed across several key domains of the RCTs.

sec.title: Statistical analyses
sec.p: The statistical analysis utilized RevMan version 5.4. To evaluate inter-study variability, we employed Cochrane’s Q test alongside I2 statistics, where values of I2 greater than 50% or p < 0.1 were interpreted as indicating substantial heterogeneity. Given the anticipated clinical heterogeneity, we applied a random-effects model for all analyses. Effect sizes were shown by standardized mean differences (SMD) with 95% CI for continuous variables, and relative risk (RR) with 95% CI for dichotomous outcomes. Sensitivity analyses were carried out to evaluate the influence of individual studies on pooled estimates. Publication bias was assessed through funnel plots and Egger’s test. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05. Additional subgroup analyses explored result stability and heterogeneity sources across surgical type, postoperative follow-up time and region to explore the stability of the results and potential sources of heterogeneity.

sec.title: Selection process and study characteristics
sec.p: The systematic literature search identified 2,316 potentially relevant records. After removing 642 duplicates, 1,674 articles underwent title and abstract screening. Of these, 1,628 were excluded based on predetermined criteria. Full-text assessment of the remaining 46 articles resulted in further exclusion of 11 due to ongoing status or incomplete data reporting. The final analysis included 35 studies [15,16,19–51] (Figure 1).

Table 1 presents the key characteristics of all included studies. The analysis encompassed 35 RCTs with a total enrollment of 3,425 participants. Individual trial sizes varied considerably, ranging from 20 to 120 subjects per study. The study population comprised patients aged 3–74 years undergoing various surgical procedures, categorized as follows: abdominal surgeries (e.g. laparoscopic cholecystectomy, laparoscopic appendectomy, laparoscopic hysterectomy, caesarean section, Inguinal hernia repair) and non-abdominal surgeries, such as thoracic procedures (e.g. thoracoscopic pneumonectomy, radical mastectomy), orthopedic surgery, genitourinary system surgery, cardiac interventional therapy, and minimally invasive diagnostic or therapeutic procedures (e.g. painless abortion, painless bronchoscopy, and magnetic resonance imaging). Esketamine was predominantly administered during the induction phase of anesthesia, while a smaller number of studies employed intraoperative continuous infusion or postoperative patient-controlled analgesia pumps. The dosing regimens exhibited considerable heterogeneity across studies. Initial bolus doses were administered at 0.1–1 mg/kg, while maintenance infusion rates during surgery ranged from 0.1 to 2 mg/kg/h. For patient-controlled analgesia, the prescribed doses varied between 0.1 and 0.5 mg/kg.

sec.p: Risk of bias assessment results are illustrated in Figure 2. High-quality methodology was evident in fourteen studies, which demonstrated minimal risk of bias. Nine studies had an unclear risk of bias due to insufficient reporting. Fifteen studies exhibited a high overall risk of bias, primarily stemming from issues related to participant and intervention provider blinding, as well as outcome assessor blinding (Figure 3).

caption.p: Risk of bias in studies included.

sec.title: Nausea and vomiting
sec.p: A total of 21 studies (701 patients) assessed nausea, while 24 studies (620 patients) evaluated vomiting. Meta-analysis showed that the control group had an increased risk of nausea (RR = 0.69, 95% CI: 0.53–0.90, p < 0.05, I2 = 78%, Figure 4) and vomiting (RR = 0.75, 95% CI: 0.57–0.98, p = 0.04, I2 = 73%, Figure S1) compared to the esketamine group.

caption.p: Forest plot showing the effect of esketamine group and control group on postoperative nausea.

sec.title: Time to first flatus
sec.p: Five studies (621 patients) reported on the time to first flatus. The esketamine group demonstrated a notably shorter time compared to control (SMD = −0.81, 95% CI: −1.48 to −0.15, p = 0.02, I2 = 93%, Figure S2), suggesting that esketamine may enhance postoperative gastrointestinal recovery.

sec.title: Rescue analgesia
sec.p: Quantitative synthesis revealed that esketamine administration was associated with decreased postoperative analgesic consumption during the initial 2-day follow-up period (RR = 0.32, 95% CI: 0.2–0.5, p < 0.05, I2 = 21%, Figure S3).

sec.title: Postoperative recovery time and PACU stay duration
sec.p: The esketamine group exhibited a longer postoperative recovery time (SMD = 0.97, 95% CI: 0.28–1.67, p < 0.05, I2 = 97%, Figure S4) and prolonged PACU stay duration (SMD = 0.76, 95% CI: 0.27–1.26, p < 0.05, I2 = 90%, Figure S5) compared to the control group.

sec.title: Other results
sec.p: Both groups exhibited comparable outcomes regarding postoperative pain scores (SMD = −0.18, 95% CI: −0.55 to −0.19, p = 0.34, I2 = 91%, Figure S6) and patient satisfaction scores (RR = 1.02, 95% CI: 0.96–1.08, p = 0.47, I2 = 23%, Figure S7). The incidence of adverse effects, including dizziness (RR = 0.97, 95% CI: 0.64–1.48, p = 0.90, I2 = 54%, Figure S8) and agitation (RR = 1.01, 95% CI: 0.64–1.60, p = 0.97, I2 = 0%, Figure S9), showed no significant between-group differences. Similarly, the meta-analysis revealed no notable differences in perioperative changes in heart rate (SMD = −0.05, 95% CI: −0.36 to 0.26, p = 0.77, I2 = 52%, Figure S10), mean arterial pressure (SMD = 0.13, 95% CI: −0.34 to 0.61), p = 0.58, I2 = 90%, Figure S11), or blood oxygen saturation (SMD = 1.03, 95% CI: −1.73 to 3.78, p = 0.73, I2 = 99%, Figure S12) during the perioperative period.

sec.title: Publication bias
sec.p: Publication bias assessment through Egger’s test and funnel plot demonstrated significant bias for nausea (Egger’s p = 0.018, Figure S13), vomiting (Egger’s p = 0.011, Figure S14), and anesthesia recovery time (Egger’s p = 0.001, Figure S15). However, no significant publication bias was detected for in postoperative pain scores (Egger’s p = 0.701, Figure S16), rescue analgesia requirements (Egger’s p = 0.472, Figure S17), PACU stay duration (Figure S18), or postoperative complications including agitation (Egger’s p = 0.101, Figure S19) and dizziness (Egger’s p = 0.880, Figure S20).

sec.title: Sensitivity analysis
sec.p: Leave-one-out sensitivity analyses confirmed the robustness of results for time to first flatus (Figure S21), postoperative nausea (Figure S22), recovery time (Figure S23), PACU stay duration (Figure S24), and postoperative pain scores (Figure S25). Nevertheless, the analysis of postoperative vomiting (Figure S26) indicated significant instability, with results becoming non-significant following the exclusion of six specific studies [32].

sec.title: Subgroup analysis
sec.p: Subgroup analyses stratified by surgical type, follow-up duration, geographic location (China versus other countries), and population (pediatric and adult) yielded notable findings. Regarding postoperative nausea, esketamine demonstrated significant efficacy at 24 h postoperatively in abdominal and orthopedic surgeries among Chinese adult populations, with no significant effect in genitourinary, thoracic, or minimally invasive surgeries. For postoperative vomiting, significant reductions were observed within 24 h in abdominal, orthopedic, and interventional surgeries among Chinese populations (including both pediatric and adult patients), with no significant effects in other surgical categories. Analysis of time to first flatus revealed substantial improvements within 24 h postoperatively across different age groups for abdominal and genitourinary surgeries, though this effect was not sustained beyond 24 h. The risk-of-bias subgroup analysis revealed that the study outcomes remained unaffected after exclusion of high-risk studies. Comprehensive results of subgroup analyses are presented in Table 2.

caption.p: Subgroup analyses of postoperative nausea, vomiting, and time to first flatus were performed according to surgical procedure (abdominal vs non-abdominal), follow-up time (within 24 h vs after 24 h), and country of use (China vs other countries), populations(pediatric vs adults), risk of bias(high vs other risk of bias).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: PONV remains a significant challenge in perioperative care. Perioperative opioid use is a key factor impairing gastrointestinal recovery; by suppressing acetylcholine release via μ-receptor activation, opioids delay gastrointestinal motility and can lead to postoperative ileus [7]. To circumvent these adverse effects, OFA has been developed as a multimodal approach that combines α-2 agonists, NMDA receptor antagonists, and local anesthetics [12,13]. However, the efficacy of OFA in preventing PONV is not unequivocal, as some studies have shown no significant reduction in its incidence[52,53]. Furthermore, regional nerve blocks have in some cases demonstrated superior efficacy to esketamine in promoting intestinal function recovery [54,55]. Given this context, esketamine, the S (+)-enantiomer of ketamine, has emerged as a promising alternative. Its higher affinity for NMDA receptors enables effective analgesia at lower doses, potentially minimizing side effects [13].

This meta-analysis demonstrates that esketamine significantly reduces postoperative nausea (RR = 0.69) and vomiting (RR = 0.75), and shortens the time to first flatus (SMD = −0.81). Notably, the esketamine group also exhibited a markedly lower demand for rescue analgesics within 48 h postoperatively (RR = 0.32). These results collectively indicate that the amelioration of PONV by esketamine is likely attributable to its potent analgesia and consequent reduction in opioid consumption. As previously mentioned, opioids disrupt gastrointestinal motility by inhibiting acetylcholine release [7,8]. Therefore, as an NMDA receptor antagonist, esketamine not only mitigates pain and central sensitization—including opioid-induced hyperalgesia [56,57]—but also indirectly facilitates gastrointestinal recovery through its opioid-sparing effect.

Subgroup analysis revealed that esketamine significantly reduced PONV within 24 h in Chinese adults undergoing abdominal and orthopedic surgeries, but not in those undergoing genitourinary, thoracic, or minimally invasive procedures. This heterogeneity can be explained by several factors. First, abdominal and orthopedic surgeries typically involve greater tissue trauma and inflammatory response, potentiating the benefit of esketamine’s anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties[36,58]. Second, the higher baseline opioid consumption in these procedures likely augments the clinical impact of esketamine’s opioid-sparing effect[16,27]. Finally, pharmacogenetic variations in esketamine metabolism or receptor sensitivity among Chinese patients may also influence its efficacy[14,29]; however, the predominance of Chinese populations in our analysis limits the generalizability of these findings.

It is important to note that esketamine was associated with a significantly prolonged anesthesia recovery time (SMD = 0.97) and PACU stay duration (SMD = 0.76). This delay likely stems from its dose-dependent sedative properties, which can prolong emergence, particularly at higher doses or with continuous infusion[29]. Transient psychotomimetic reactions (e.g. dream-like states or dissociation) may also necessitate extended monitoring in the PACU [59], a situation potentially exacerbated by conservative discharge protocols in the presence of such side effects [32]. Notwithstanding these drawbacks, for high-risk PONV patients—such as those undergoing abdominal surgery or with opioid dependence—the substantial benefits of esketamine in reducing PONV and enhancing gastrointestinal recovery may well offset the disadvantage of a longer immediate recovery. By preventing complications like dehydration, aspiration pneumonia, and delayed ambulation, esketamine could potentially facilitate shorter overall hospital stays and improve the patient experience [1,60]. Consequently, in fast-track settings such as day surgery or enhanced recovery after surgery pathways, we recommend an individualized approach. This involves careful patient selection and optimization of esketamine dosing and timing to strike a balance between its sedative profile and its gastrointestinal benefits.

This meta-analysis has several limitations. Firstly, the relatively small sample size of included RCTs may introduce selection bias. Secondly, geographical limitation to Chinese populations necessitates future multi-center, large-sample studies incorporating diverse regions and ethnicities. Finally, methodological concerns arose from 12 of 38 included RCTs lacking clear allocation concealment and blinding procedures. While certain outcomes showed low publication bias risks, significant bias was detected for postoperative nausea, vomiting, and anesthesia recovery time, with notably unstable sensitivity analyses for postoperative vomiting. Nevertheless, this study represents the most recent meta-analysis examining esketamine’s role in postoperative gastrointestinal function regulation, offering high-quality evidence through RCTs and identifying optimal surgical applications through comprehensive subgroup analyses.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This meta-analysis suggests that perioperative administration of esketamine may potentially reduce PONV and accelerate the time to first flatus. Although esketamine-based opioid-sparing anesthesia holds clinical promise, the notable heterogeneity and relatively small sample sizes in the included studies highlight the need for more robust investigations to confirm its preventive effects on postoperative gastrointestinal dysfunction. Despite our meta-analysis supporting esketamine’s modest benefits for PONV reduction and gastrointestinal function recovery, several critical knowledge gaps persist: 1) Optimal dosing and timing: Future RCTs should compare bolus with continuous infusion regimens and evaluate intraoperative versus postoperative administration. 2) Patient stratification: Research should focus on targeting high-risk cohorts (e.g. opioid-dependent patients, individuals undergoing major abdominal surgery) to refine the risk-benefit assessment of esketamine. 3) Mechanistic validation: Translational research integrating gut motility measurements (e.g. wireless motility capsule testing) with biomarkers (e.g. serum IL-6, fecal calprotectin) could clarify esketamine’s ENS function. 4) Multimodal protocols: The potential synergy between esketamine with other OFA adjuncts (e.g. dexmedetomidine, lidocaine) warrants investigation to further reduce delays in postoperative recovery.

sec.title: Ethics approval
sec.p: Not applicable

sec.title: Consent to participate
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Consent to publish
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: The authors have no conflict of interest to declare.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: The authors confirm that the data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article and its supplementary materials.
